text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Amelia Hart
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Amelia Hart
Consultant Neurologist,  

Daniel Carter, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: KCH918472 NHS No. 7465203891  
12 Rowan Mews, London, SE15 3HT

Thank you for referring this patient for review of ongoing seizures and treatment optimisation. He attended with his partner, who provided collateral history. The clinical picture and prior documentation support a diagnosis of Combined Generalised and Focal Epilepsy Type, with clear evidence of both generalised and focal epilepsy. He reports brief morning myoclonic jerks occurring intermittently, and separate focal impaired-awareness seizures characterised by behavioural arrest, fixed gaze, automatisms of the right hand, and post‑event confusion lasting approximately 20–30 minutes.

Regarding current seizure burden, he describes 2 - 3 clusters this month; each ~five focal impaired-awareness seizures. Prior to trialling current therapy, he also experienced clusters at a similar rate; despite adherence and no missed doses reported, the frequency has remained unchanged. He notes occasional probable generalised tonic–clonic seizures from sleep about twice per year, the last being in late June. Potential triggers include poor sleep and missed meals; alcohol intake is modest and stable. There is no recent head injury, intercurrent infection, or new medication.

He is taking levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily and lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily without reported rash, mood change, or dose-limiting adverse effects. Folic acid is continued. Past treatments included sodium valproate (weight gain, discontinued) and lacosamide (dizziness). There is no history of status epilepticus. He does not drive and is aware of DVLA guidance. Occupationally he works part‑time from home and uses a seizure diary app, which corroborates the clustering pattern described above.

Neurological examination today was unremarkable between events. Prior MRI (2023) was structurally normal; prior routine EEG showed generalised spike–wave discharges as well as left temporal sharp waves, supporting the combined generalised and focal epilepsy phenotype. Routine bloods from last month (U&Es, LFTs, FBC) were within reference ranges.

Plan discussed: I have arranged an ambulatory EEG with video capture to better characterise the focal impaired‑awareness seizures within clusters and to sample for generalised discharges. We will also repeat antiseizure medication levels where applicable and check vitamin D. I provided information on sleep regularisation, hydration, and diary‑based trigger tracking. A rescue plan has been reiterated for prolonged clusters, and his partner feels confident with the agreed thresholds for seeking urgent assistance. Follow‑up will be in 8–10 weeks, sooner if there is any change in pattern or injury. 

Thank you for your referral and ongoing care. Please let me know if there are specific concerns you wish me to address at the next review.

Yours sincerely

attributes.Label: 2 to 3 cluster per month, 5 per cluster

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit current frequency: The letter states, "he describes 2 - 3 clusters this month; each ~five focal impaired-awareness seizures." This provides a numeric range of clusters per month and number per cluster. Other seizure types mentioned include probable generalized tonic–clonic seizures about twice per year, but per rule 7 we select the highest frequency, which is the clustered focal impaired-awareness seizures monthly. Normalize to allowed format: ranges use 'to' and singular units. Therefore: '2 to 3 cluster per month, 5 per cluster'.
seizure_frequency_number: 2 to 3 cluster per month, 5 per cluster
